summary: DeVault reflects on his mixed track record spending money on open hardware projects, noting several that became vaporware (EOMA68, DragonBox Pyra, Jolla Tablet) alongside successes like Pine64 and HiFive Unmatched. He observes that even successful open hardware products often contain non-free components and suffer stock issues. Rather than offering definitive answers, he calls for mentorship between successful and emerging projects, a stronger push to liberate non-free components, and greater transparency from project creators. He places responsibility on both producers and consumers to be more discerning about project viability.
key_points: Many open hardware projects have failed to deliver, becoming vaporware or seriously under-delivering on promises
Even the success stories (Pine64, Raspberry Pi, Arduino) come with significant caveats like non-free components and poor stock availability
The open hardware movement needs structural support: mentorship from successful projects, investment platforms like Crowd Supply, and greater transparency
Consumers share responsibility by directing spending toward genuinely free projects and critically evaluating project viability before backing them
money_quotes: They say a sucker is born every day, and at least on the day of my birth, that certainly may have been true.
I have a bad habit of spending money on open hardware projects that ultimately become vaporware or seriously under-deliver on their expectations.
What does open hardware need to succeed, and why have many projects failed? And why do the successful products often have non-free components and poor stock?
I don't know the answers, but I hope we start seeing improvements.
Part of this responsibility comes down to the consumer: spend your money on free projects, and don't spend your money on non-free projects.
We also need to look closely at the viability of each project, and open hardware projects need to be transparent about their plans, lest we get burned again.
Steering the open hardware movement out of infancy will be a challenge for all involved.
key_insight: The open hardware movement remains in its infancy, with even its successes riddled with non-free compromises, and maturing will require mentorship, transparency, and more discerning consumer spending.
